claims.text: The Adam paper (Kingma & Ba, 2014) specifies good default settings for the tested machine learning problems as β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999 and ε = 10^(−8), presented as paper-wide recommended defaults accompanying Algorithm 1.
claims.verification_reason: The passage directly states "Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are α = 0.001, β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999 and ε = 10^(−8)" as part of Algorithm 1, supporting every qualifier.

claims.text: The Transformer paper (Vaswani et al., 2017) reports using the Adam optimizer with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.98 and ε = 10^(−9) in its optimizer-configuration description.
claims.verification_reason: The passage directly states "We used the Adam optimizer [20] with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.98 and ε = 10^(−9)" in section 5.3 Optimizer, supporting the exact wording.

claims.text: The Adam paper's β2 = 0.999 is described as a paper-wide recommended default for the tested machine learning problems, whereas the Transformer paper reports β2 = 0.98 as the Adam setting it used in its experiments. Since one value is a general suggested default and the other is an experimental setting, they are not in direct conflict.
claims.verification_reason: The Adam passage directly supports "paper-wide recommended default for the tested machine learning problems." The Transformer passage supports that β2=0.98 is what "We used," indicating an experimental setting. Rewritten to avoid adding "chosen for training the Transformer model" framing not literally in the passage.

items.span_text:
Algorithm 1: Adam, our proposed algorithm for stochastic optimization. See section 2 for details,
and for a slightly more efficient (but less clear) order of computation. g _ { t } ^ { 2 } indicates the elementwise
square g _ { t } \odot g _ { t } . Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are \alpha = 0 . 0 0 1
\bar { \beta _ { 1 } } = 0 \bar { . } 9 , \beta _ { 2 } \bar { = } 0 . 999 and \epsilon = 1 0 ^ { - 8 } . All operations on vectors are element-wise. With \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } and \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t }
we denote \beta _ { 1 } and \beta _ { 2 } to the power t .
Require: \alpha \mathrm { : } Stepsize
Require: \beta _ { 1 } , \bar { \beta _ { 2 } } \overset { - } { \in } [ 0 , 1 ) : Exponential decay rates for the moment estimates
Require: f ( \theta ) { \mathrm { : } } Stochastic objective function with parameters θ
Require: \theta _ { 0 } \colon Initial parameter vector
m _ { 0 } \gets 0 (Initialize 1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } } moment vector)
v _ { 0 } 0 (Initialize 2 ^ { \mathrm { n d } } moment vector)
t \gets 0 (Initialize timestep)
while \theta _ { t } not converged do
t \gets t + 1
g _ { t } \gets \nabla _ { \theta } f _ { t } ( \theta _ { t - 1 } ) (Get gradients w.r.t. stochastic objective at timestep t)
m _ { t } \gets \beta _ { 1 } \cdot m _ { t - 1 } \dot { + } \left( 1 \bar { - } \beta _ { 1 } \right) \cdot g _ { t } (Update biased first moment estimate)
v _ { t } \beta _ { 2 } \cdot v _ { t - 1 } + ( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ) \cdot g _ { t } ^ { 2 } (Update biased second raw moment estimate)
\widehat { m } _ { t } \gets m _ { t } / ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } ) (Compute bias-corrected first moment estimate)
\widehat { v } _ { t } \gets v _ { t } / ( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t } ) (Compute bias-corrected second raw moment estimate)
\theta _ { t } \theta _ { t - 1 } - \alpha \cdot \widehat { m } _ { t } / ( \sqrt { v _ { t } } + \epsilon ) (Update parameters)
end while
return { \underline { { \theta _ { t } } } } (Resulting parameters)
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

items.span_text: The algorithm updates exponential moving averages of the gradient $( m _ { t } )$ and the squared gradient $( v _ { t } )$ where the hyper-parameters $\beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } \in [ 0 , 1 )$ control the exponential decay rates of these moving averages. The moving averages themselves are estimates of the $1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } }$ moment (the mean) and the $2 ^ { \mathrm { n d } }$ raw moment (the uncentered variance) of the gradient. However, these moving averages are initialized as (vectors of) $0 ^ { \cdot } \mathrm { s } ,$ leading to moment estimates that are biased towards zero, especially during the initial timesteps, and especially when the decay rates are small (i.e. the $\beta \mathbf { s }$ are close to 1). The good news is that this initialization bias can be easily counteracted, resulting in bias-corrected estimates $\widehat { m } _ { t }$ and $\widehat { v } _ { t } .$ . See section 3 for more details.
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

items.span_text: We used the Adam optimizer [20] with $\beta _ { 1 } = 0 . 9 , \beta _ { 2 } = 0 . 9 8$ and $\epsilon = 1 0 ^ { - 9 }$ . We varied the learning rate over the course of training, according to the formula:
items.title: Attention Is All You Need

items.span_text: As explained in section 2, Adam utilizes initialization bias correction terms. We will here derive the term for the second moment estimate; the derivation for the first moment estimate is completely analogous. Let $g$ be the gradient of the stochastic objective $f ,$ and we wish to estimate its second raw moment (uncentered variance) using an exponential moving average of the squared gradient, with decay rate $\beta _ { 2 }$ . Let $g _ { 1 } , . . . , g _ { T }$ be the gradients at subsequent timesteps, each a draw from an underlying gradient distribution $g _ { t } \sim p ( g _ { t } )$ . Let us initialize the exponential moving average as $v _ { 0 } = 0$ (a vector of zeros). First note that the update at timestep t of the exponential moving average $v _ { t } = \beta _ { 2 } \cdot v _ { t - 1 } + ( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ) \cdot g _ { t } ^ { 2 }$ (where $g _ { t } ^ { 2 }$ indicates the elementwise square $g _ { t } \odot g _ { t } )$ can be written as a function of the gradients at all previous timesteps:
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

items.span_text:
Table 2: The Transformer achieves better BLEU scores than previous state-of-the-art models on the English-to-German and English-to-French newstest2014 tests at a fraction of the training cost.
Model | BLEU | Training Cost (FLOPs) | EN-DE | EN-FR | EN-DE | EN-FR | ByteNet [18] | 23.75 | | | | Deep-Att + PosUnk [39] | | 39.2 | | $1 . 0 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | GNMT + RL [38] | 24.6 | 39.92 | $2 . 3 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ | $1 . 4 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | ConvS2S [9] | 25.16 | 40.46 | $9 . 6 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 8 }$ | $1 . 5 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | MoE [32] | 26.03 | 40.56 | $2 . 0 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ | $1 . 2 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | Deep-Att + PosUnk Ensemble [39] | | 40.4 | | $\overline { { 8 . 0 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 } } }$ | $\mathrm { G N M T } + \mathrm { R I }$ Ensemble [38] | 26.30 | 41.16 | $1 . 8 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | $1 . 1 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 1 }$ | ConvS2S Ensemble [9] | 26.36 | 41.29 | $7 . 7 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ | $1 . 2 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 1 }$ | Transformer (base model) | 27.3 | 38.1 | $\mathbf { 3 . 3 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 8 } }$ | | Transformer (big) | 28.4 | 41.8 | $2 . 3 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ |
items.title: Attention Is All You Need

items.span_text:
Algorithm 2: AdaMax, a variant of Adam based on the infinity norm. See section 7.1 for details.
Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are \alpha \ : = \ : 0 . 0 0 2 , \beta _ { 1 } \ : = \ : 0 . 9 and
\beta _ { 2 } = 0 . 9 9 9 . With \beta _ { 1 } ^ { \overline { { t } } } we denote \beta _ { 1 } to the power t. Here, ( \alpha / ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } ) ) is the learning rate with the
bias-correction term for the first moment. All operations on vectors are element-wise.
Require: \alpha \mathrm { : } Stepsize
Require: \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } \^ { \mathrm { ~ \bullet ~ } } ( 0 , 1 ) \colon Exponential decay rates
Require: f ( \theta ) { \mathrm { : } } : Stochastic objective function with parameters \theta
Require: \theta _ { 0 } \colon Initial parameter vector
m _ { 0 } \gets 0 (Initialize 1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } } moment vector)
u _ { 0 } \gets 0 (Initialize the exponentially weighted infinity norm)
t \gets 0 (Initialize timestep)
while \theta _ { t } not converged do
t \gets t + 1
g _ { t } \gets \nabla _ { \theta } f _ { t } ( \theta _ { t - 1 } ) (Get gradients w.r.t. stochastic objective at timestep t)
m _ { t } \gets \beta _ { 1 } \cdot m _ { t - 1 } + ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ) \cdot g _ { t } (Update biased first moment estimate)
u _ { t } \gets max ( \beta _ { 2 } \cdot u _ { t - 1 } , | g _ { t } | ) (Update the exponentially weighted infinity norm)
\theta _ { t } \theta _ { t - 1 } - ( \alpha / ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } ) ) \cdot m _ { t } / u _ { t } (Update parameters)
end while
return \theta _ { t } (Resulting parameters)
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

items.span_text: where $\zeta = 0$ if the true second moment $\mathbb { E } [ g _ { i } ^ { 2 } ]$ is stationary; otherwise $\zeta$ can be kept small since the exponential decay rate $\beta _ { 1 }$ can (and should) be chosen such that the exponential moving average assigns small weights to gradients too far in the past. What is left is the term $( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t } )$ which is caused by initializing the running average with zeros. In algorithm 1 we therefore divide by this term to correct the initialization bias.
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

fields.scope_distinction: Adam: paper-wide recommended default for tested problems vs. Transformer: Adam setting used in its experiments; distinguishes a general default from an experimental setting.

final_answer.markdown:
- **Source A:** The Adam paper (Kingma & Ba, 2014) specifies good default settings for the tested machine learning problems as β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999 and ε = 10^(−8), presented as paper-wide recommended defaults accompanying Algorithm 1. [1]
- **Source B:** The Transformer paper (Vaswani et al., 2017) reports using the Adam optimizer with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.98 and ε = 10^(−9) in its optimizer-configuration description. [2]
- **Resolution:** The Adam paper's β2 = 0.999 is described as a paper-wide recommended default for the tested machine learning problems, whereas the Transformer paper reports β2 = 0.98 as the Adam setting it used in its experiments. Since one value is a general suggested default and the other is an experimental setting, they are not in direct conflict. [2]

Sources
[1] Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization, unsectioned, p. 2
[2] Attention Is All You Need, 5.3 Optimizer, p. 7
final_answer.summary: The Adam paper (Kingma & Ba, 2014) specifies good default settings for the tested machine learning problems as β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999 and ε = 10^(−8), presented as paper-wide recommended defaults accompanying Algorithm 1.

constraints.coverage_assumption: User is referring to the Adam optimizer paper (Kingma & Ba, 2014) and the original Transformer paper (Vaswani et al., 2017); both are present in the corpus.

task.object: conflict about Adam beta2 between sources

intent_frame.normalized_question: Resolve the apparent conflict about Adam's β2 by retrieving what the Adam paper specifies versus what the Transformer paper actually uses, and explain why both sources appear to disagree.

obligations.description: What value of β2 does the Adam paper (Kingma & Ba, 2014) report as its default or recommended setting?
What value of β2 does the Transformer paper (Vaswani et al., 2017) report using for Adam in its optimizer configuration?
What scope/context in each paper does the β2 value appear in (e.g., paper-wide default vs. specific model/optimizer-table entry), and does that distinction explain the apparent conflict?

intent_frame.raw_question: Some sources say Adam beta2 is 0.999, but the Transformer paper used 0.98. Resolve this conflict.

Model | BLEU | Training Cost (FLOPs) | EN-DE | EN-FR | EN-DE | EN-FR | ByteNet [18] | 23.75 | | | | Deep-Att + PosUnk [39] | | 39.2 | | $1 . 0 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | GNMT + RL [38] | 24.6 | 39.92 | $2 . 3 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ | $1 . 4 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | ConvS2S [9] | 25.16 | 40.46 | $9 . 6 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 8 }$ | $1 . 5 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | MoE [32] | 26.03 | 40.56 | $2 . 0 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ | $1 . 2 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | Deep-Att + PosUnk Ensemble [39] | | 40.4 | | $\overline { { 8 . 0 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 } } }$ | $\mathrm { G N M T } + \mathrm { R I }$ Ensemble [38] | 26.30 | 41.16 | $1 . 8 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 0 }$ | $1 . 1 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 1 }$ | ConvS2S Ensemble [9] | 26.36 | 41.29 | $7 . 7 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ | $1 . 2 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 2 1 }$ | Transformer (base model) | 27.3 | 38.1 | $\mathbf { 3 . 3 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 8 } }$ | | Transformer (big) | 28.4 | 41.8 | $2 . 3 \cdot 1 0 ^ { 1 9 }$ |
items.title: Attention Is All You Need

items.span_text:
Algorithm 2: AdaMax, a variant of Adam based on the infinity norm. See section 7.1 for details.
Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are \alpha \ : = \ : 0 . 0 0 2 , \beta _ { 1 } \ : = \ : 0 . 9 and
\beta _ { 2 } = 0 . 9 9 9 . With \beta _ { 1 } ^ { \overline { { t } } } we denote \beta _ { 1 } to the power t. Here, ( \alpha / ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } ) ) is the learning rate with the
bias-correction term for the first moment. All operations on vectors are element-wise.
Require: \alpha \mathrm { : } Stepsize
Require: \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } \^ { \mathrm { ~ \bullet ~ } } ( 0 , 1 ) \colon Exponential decay rates
Require: f ( \theta ) { \mathrm { : } } : Stochastic objective function with parameters \theta
Require: \theta _ { 0 } \colon Initial parameter vector
m _ { 0 } \gets 0 (Initialize 1 ^ { \mathrm { s t } } moment vector)
u _ { 0 } \gets 0 (Initialize the exponentially weighted infinity norm)
t \gets 0 (Initialize timestep)
while \theta _ { t } not converged do
t \gets t + 1
g _ { t } \gets \nabla _ { \theta } f _ { t } ( \theta _ { t - 1 } ) (Get gradients w.r.t. stochastic objective at timestep t)
m _ { t } \gets \beta _ { 1 } \cdot m _ { t - 1 } + ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ) \cdot g _ { t } (Update biased first moment estimate)
u _ { t } \gets max ( \beta _ { 2 } \cdot u _ { t - 1 } , | g _ { t } | ) (Update the exponentially weighted infinity norm)
\theta _ { t } \theta _ { t - 1 } - ( \alpha / ( 1 - \beta _ { 1 } ^ { t } ) ) \cdot m _ { t } / u _ { t } (Update parameters)
end while
return \theta _ { t } (Resulting parameters)
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

items.span_text: where $\zeta = 0$ if the true second moment $\mathbb { E } [ g _ { i } ^ { 2 } ]$ is stationary; otherwise $\zeta$ can be kept small since the exponential decay rate $\beta _ { 1 }$ can (and should) be chosen such that the exponential moving average assigns small weights to gradients too far in the past. What is left is the term $( 1 - \beta _ { 2 } ^ { t } )$ which is caused by initializing the running average with zeros. In algorithm 1 we therefore divide by this term to correct the initialization bias.
items.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

stage_trace.decision_summary: Verified 3 atomic claim(s): 2 kept, 1 rewritten, 0 dropped.

claim_verdicts.reason: The passage directly states "Good default settings for the tested machine learning problems are α = 0.001, β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999 and ε = 10^(−8)" as part of Algorithm 1, supporting every qualifier.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: The Adam paper (Kingma & Ba, 2014) specifies good default settings for the tested machine learning problems as β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999 and ε = 10^(−8), presented as paper-wide recommended defaults accompanying Algorithm 1.

claim_verdicts.reason: The passage directly states "We used the Adam optimizer [20] with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.98 and ε = 10^(−9)" in section 5.3 Optimizer, supporting the exact wording.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: The Transformer paper (Vaswani et al., 2017) reports using the Adam optimizer with β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.98 and ε = 10^(−9) in its optimizer-configuration description.

field_values.scope_distinction: Adam: paper-wide recommended default for tested problems vs. Transformer: Adam setting used in its experiments; distinguishes a general default from an experimental setting.

claim_verdicts.reason: The Adam passage directly supports "paper-wide recommended default for the tested machine learning problems." The Transformer passage supports that β2=0.98 is what "We used," indicating an experimental setting. Rewritten to avoid adding "chosen for training the Transformer model" framing not literally in the passage.
claim_verdicts.verified_text: The Adam paper's β2 = 0.999 is described as a paper-wide recommended default for the tested machine learning problems, whereas the Transformer paper reports β2 = 0.98 as the Adam setting it used in its experiments. Since one value is a general suggested default and the other is an experimental setting, they are not in direct conflict.
